title: Getting Started
text: need Miniconda. Download installer OS link follow -screen steps.

title: Getting Started
text: Open Terminal run:
code: git clone https://github.com/allesgrau/volaris.git cd volaris brew install gsl libomp conda create -n volaris python=3.11 conda activate volaris conda install -c conda-forge numpy cython cd python pip install -e .

title: Getting Started
text: Open Terminal run:
code: git clone https://github.com/allesgrau/volaris.git cd volaris sudo apt-get install libgsl-dev conda create -n volaris python=3.11 conda activate volaris conda install -c conda-forge numpy cython cd python pip install -e .

title: Getting Started
text: Open Anaconda Prompt (installed Miniconda) run:
code: git clone https://github.com/allesgrau/volaris.git cd volaris conda create -n volaris python=3.11 conda activate volaris conda install -c conda-forge gsl numpy cython cd python pip install -e .

title: Getting Started
code: import volaris print(volaris.bs_price(100, 100, 1.0, 0.05, 0.2, 1))  # ~10.45

title: Getting Started
text: Open Terminal run: R: Terminal:
code: git clone https://github.com/allesgrau/volaris.git cd volaris brew install r gsl install.packages("Rcpp") R CMD INSTALL R/Volaris

title: Getting Started
text: Open Terminal run: R: Terminal:
code: git clone https://github.com/allesgrau/volaris.git cd volaris sudo apt-get install r-base libgsl-dev install.packages("Rcpp") R CMD INSTALL R/Volaris

title: Getting Started
text: Install Rtools: choose version matching R version (e.g. Rtools45 R 4.5.x). Download run installer. Open Rtools Bash: press Start, search Rtools, open Rtools Bash (terminal window). Rtools Bash, install GSL: Clone repository (still Rtools Bash): Open R install required R package: Back PowerShell (regular Windows terminal), repo root:
code: pacman -S mingw-w64-ucrt-x86_64-gsl git clone https://github.com/allesgrau/volaris.git cd volaris install.packages("Rcpp") R CMD INSTALL R\Volaris

title: Examples (Python)
text: notebook demonstrates selection capabilities Volaris library: - option pricing Black-Scholes model - Greeks calculation: delta, gamma, vega, theta, rho - fast root-finding numerical integration compiled C core - binomial tree discrete alternative Black-Scholes
code: import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt  from volaris import (     bs_price, bs_delta, bs_gamma, bs_vega, bs_theta, bs_rho,     binomial_price,     rootfind_newton, rootfind_bisect,     integrate_gauss, integrate_gsl,     implied_vol, )  S, K, T, r, sigma = 100.0, 100.0, 1.0, 0.05, 0.20

title: Examples (Python)
text: Black-Scholes model assumes underlying option follows Geometric Brownian Motion. Variables used: | Symbol | Meaning | Example value | |——–|———|—————| | S | current price underlying asset | 100.0 | | K | strike price (exercise price option) | 100.0 | | T | time expiration years | 1.0 (= 1 year) | | r | risk-free interest rate (annualised) | 0.05 (= 5%) | | sigma | volatility underlying (annualised) | 0.20 (= 20%) | | C | call option price: right buy K | computed | | P | put option price: right sell K | computed | Call put prices --money option, verified via put-call parity:
code: call = bs_price(S, K, T, r, sigma, 1) put  = bs_price(S, K, T, r, sigma, 0)  print(f"Call: {call:.4f}") print(f"Put:  {put:.4f}")  print(f"Put-call parity check: {call - put:.4f} vs {S - K * np.exp(-r * T):.4f}") if abs((call - put) - (S - K * np.exp(-r * T))) < 1e-10:     print("Put-call parity holds!") Call: 10.4506 Put:  5.5735 Put-call parity check: 4.8771 vs 4.8771 Put-call parity holds!

title: Examples (Python)
text: Greeks measure sensitivity option price market parameters. : delta, gamma, vega, theta call put options function spot price S.
code: spots = np.linspace(60, 140, 200)  deltas_c = [bs_delta(s, K, T, r, sigma, 1) for s in spots] deltas_p = [bs_delta(s, K, T, r, sigma, 0) for s in spots] gammas = [bs_gamma(s, K, T, r, sigma) for s in spots] vegas = [bs_vega(s, K, T, r, sigma) for s in spots] thetas_c = [bs_theta(s, K, T, r, sigma, 1) for s in spots] thetas_p = [bs_theta(s, K, T, r, sigma, 0) for s in spots] fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 8))  axes[0,0].plot(spots, deltas_c, label="call") axes[0,0].plot(spots, deltas_p, label="put") axes[0,0].set_title("Delta"); axes[0,0].legend() axes[0,0].axvline(K, ls="--", c="gray")  axes[0,1].plot(spots, gammas, color="green") axes[0,1].set_title("Gamma") axes[0,1].axvline(K, ls="--", c="gray")  axes[1,0].plot(spots, vegas, color="red") axes[1,0].set_title("Vega") axes[1,0].axvline(K, ls="--", c="gray")  axes[1,1].plot(spots, thetas_c, label="call") axes[1,1].plot(spots, thetas_p, label="put") axes[1,1].set_title("Theta"); axes[1,1].legend() axes[1,1].axvline(K, ls="--", c="gray")  for ax in axes.flat:     ax.set_xlabel("S");     ax.grid(alpha=0.3)  plt.suptitle("Greeks for an ATM option (K=100, T=1, r=5%, sigma=20%)") plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

title: Examples (Python)
text: Volaris provides two root-finding methods implemented C. Example: find sigma BS call price equals 15. also exactly implied_vol internally.
code: target = 15.0  f  = lambda sigma: bs_price(S, K, T, r, sigma, 1) - target df = lambda sigma: bs_vega(S, K, T, r, sigma)  root_newton = rootfind_newton(f, df, x0=0.3) root_bisect = rootfind_bisect(f, 0.01, 2.0) iv = implied_vol(target, S, K, T, r, 1)  print(f"Target: {target}") print("------------------") print(f"Newton-Raphson:  sigma = {root_newton:.6f}  |  price = {bs_price(S, K, T, r, root_newton, 1):.6f}") print(f"Bisection:       sigma = {root_bisect:.6f}  |  price = {bs_price(S, K, T, r, root_bisect, 1):.6f}") print(f"Implied vol:     sigma = {iv:.6f}  |  price = {bs_price(S, K, T, r, iv, 1):.6f}") Target: 15.0 ------------------ Newton-Raphson:  sigma = 0.320258  |  price = 15.000000 Bisection:       sigma = 0.320258  |  price = 15.000000 Implied vol:     sigma = 0.320258  |  price = 15.000000

title: Examples (Python)
text: Black-Scholes formula derived integral normal distribution. Comparison Gauss quadrature (custom C implementation) GSL QAGS adaptive integration:
code: from math import pi, exp, sqrt  def normal_pdf(x):    return exp(-0.5 * x * x) / sqrt(2 * pi)  a, b = -3.0, 3.0 exact = 0.9973002039  # P(-3 < Z < 3)  result_gauss = integrate_gauss(normal_pdf, a, b, n_points=20) result_gsl = integrate_gsl(normal_pdf, a, b, tol=1e-10)  print(f"Gauss quadrature (n=20): {result_gauss:.10f}  |  error: {abs(result_gauss - exact):.2e}") print(f"GSL QAGS:                {result_gsl:.10f}  |  error: {abs(result_gsl - exact):.2e}") print(f"Exact value:             {exact:.10f}") Gauss quadrature (n=20): 0.9973002039  |  error: 3.67e-11 GSL QAGS:                0.9973002039  |  error: 3.67e-11 Exact value:             0.9973002039

what: Options pricing with a binomial tree
title: Examples (Python)
text: Cox-Ross-Rubinstein model demonstrates Brownian motion binomial tree. number steps N increases, price converges Black-Scholes analytical value:
code: bs_ref = bs_price(S, K, T, r, sigma, 1) steps  = [5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500] prices = [binomial_price(S, K, T, r, 0.0, sigma, N, 1, 0) for N in steps] plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4)) plt.semilogx(steps, prices, "o-", label="Binomial tree") plt.axhline(bs_ref, color="red", ls="--", label=f"Black-Scholes = {bs_ref:.4f}") plt.xlabel("Number of steps N") plt.ylabel("Call option price") plt.title("Convergence of CRR binomial tree to Black-Scholes") plt.legend() plt.grid(alpha=0.3) plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

title: Examples (Python)
text: notebook demonstrates selection capabilities Volaris library : - finding implied volatility smile surface via Black-Scholes model inversion - volatility calculation: Close--Close, Parkinson, Garman-Klass, Yang-Zhang estimators - comparison estimator efficiency simulated OHLC data
code: import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt  from volaris import (     bs_price,      hist_vol_close_to_close, hist_vol_parkinson, hist_vol_garman_klass, hist_vol_yang_zhang,     gbm_paths,     implied_vol, )  S, r, sigma = 100.0, 0.05, 0.20 rng = np.random.default_rng(123)

title: Examples (Python)
text: Black-Scholes world, IV flat across strikes. practice markets show volatility smile: OTM puts calls trade higher IV ATM. simulate smile pricing options strike-dependent volatility, recovering implied volatility prices using Newton-Raphson / bisection inversion.
code: T = 1.0 strikes = np.linspace(50, 250, 50)  def smile_sigma(K):     m = (K - S) / S     return 0.20 - 0.05 * m + 0.10 * m**2  market_prices = np.array([bs_price(S, K, T, r, smile_sigma(K), 1) for K in strikes]) ivs = np.array([implied_vol(p, S, K, T, r, 1) for p, K in zip(market_prices, strikes)]) true_sigmas = np.array([smile_sigma(K) for K in strikes]) plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5)) plt.plot(strikes, true_sigmas * 100, "k--", label="true σ(K)") plt.plot(strikes, ivs * 100, "o", ms=4, label="recovered IV") plt.xlabel("Strike K") plt.ylabel("Implied volatility (%)") plt.title("Volatility smile (T = 1 year)") plt.legend(); plt.grid(alpha=0.3) plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

title: Examples (Python)
text: Extending smile across multiple maturities gives volatility surface. term structure volatility reflects market expectations different horizons.
code: tenors  = np.array([0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0]) strikes_grid = np.linspace(75, 125, 30)  surface = np.zeros((len(tenors), len(strikes_grid)))  for i, T_ in enumerate(tenors):     for j, K in enumerate(strikes_grid):         m = (K - S) / S         vol = 0.20 - 0.05 * m + 0.10 * m**2 + 0.02 * np.sqrt(T_)         price = bs_price(S, K, T_, r, vol, 1 if K >= S else 0)         surface[i, j] = implied_vol(price, S, K, T_, r, 1 if K >= S else 0) KK, TT = np.meshgrid(strikes_grid, tenors) fig = plt.figure(figsize=(8, 8)) ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d") ax.plot_surface(KK, TT, surface * 100, cmap="viridis", alpha=0.85) ax.set_xlabel("Strike K");  ax.set_ylabel("Maturity T (years)") ax.set_zlabel("Implied vol (%)") ax.set_title("Implied Volatility Surface") plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

title: Examples (Python)
text: Volaris implements four classical estimators realised volatility, computed C OHLC (Open, High, Low, Close) price data. simulate 252 days OHLC data GBM known sigma = 20%.
code: true_sigma = 0.20 N_days = 252  # Simulate intraday paths (open, high, low, close per day)  intraday_steps = 100 paths = gbm_paths(S0=100.0, mu=0.05, sigma=true_sigma, T=1.0, N_steps=N_days * intraday_steps, N_paths=1) prices = paths[0] days = prices.reshape(N_days, intraday_steps) open_ = days[:, 0].copy() close_ = days[:, -1].copy() high_ = days.max(axis=1) low_ = days.min(axis=1)  returns = np.diff(np.log(close_)) returns = np.ascontiguousarray(returns) open_ = np.ascontiguousarray(open_[1:]) close_ = np.ascontiguousarray(close_[1:]) high_ = np.ascontiguousarray(high_[1:]) low_ = np.ascontiguousarray(low_[1:]) ctc = hist_vol_close_to_close(returns) park = hist_vol_parkinson(high_, low_) gk = hist_vol_garman_klass(high_, low_, open_, close_) yz = hist_vol_yang_zhang(high_, low_, open_, close_)  print(f"True sigma:     {true_sigma:.4f}") print("---------------") print(f"Close-to-Close: {ctc:.4f}") print(f"Parkinson:      {park:.4f}") print(f"Garman-Klass:   {gk:.4f}") print(f"Yang-Zhang:     {yz:.4f}") True sigma:     0.2000 --------------- Close-to-Close: 0.1855 Parkinson:      0.1780 Garman-Klass:   0.1756 Yang-Zhang:     0.1789

title: Examples (Python)
text: Computing volatility rolling 21-day window shows estimator tracks changing market conditions time.
code: window = 21 n = len(returns)  ctc_roll, park_roll, gk_roll, yz_roll = [], [], [], []  for start in range(n - window):     sl = slice(start, start + window)     ctc_roll.append(hist_vol_close_to_close(np.ascontiguousarray(returns[sl])))     park_roll.append(hist_vol_parkinson(np.ascontiguousarray(high_[sl]), np.ascontiguousarray(low_[sl])))     gk_roll.append(hist_vol_garman_klass(np.ascontiguousarray(high_[sl]), np.ascontiguousarray(low_[sl]),                                           np.ascontiguousarray(open_[sl]), np.ascontiguousarray(close_[sl])))     yz_roll.append(hist_vol_yang_zhang(np.ascontiguousarray(high_[sl]), np.ascontiguousarray(low_[sl]),                                        np.ascontiguousarray(open_[sl]), np.ascontiguousarray(close_[sl]))) plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5)) plt.plot(ctc_roll, label="Close-to-Close", alpha=0.7) plt.plot(park_roll, label="Parkinson", alpha=0.7) plt.plot(gk_roll, label="Garman-Klass", alpha=0.7) plt.plot(yz_roll, label="Yang-Zhang", alpha=0.7) plt.axhline(true_sigma, color="black", ls="--", label=f"True σ = {true_sigma}") plt.xlabel("Day"); plt.ylabel("Annualised volatility") plt.title("Rolling 21-day historical volatility (GBM simulation)") plt.legend(); plt.grid(alpha=0.3) plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

title: Examples (Python)
text: notebook demonstrates selection capabilities Volaris library: - European, Asian, barrier option pricing via Monte Carlo simulation - GBM path simulation antithetic variates variance reduction - Merton jump-diffusion model (GBM + compound Poisson jumps) - GARCH(1,1) volatility modelling forecasting
code: import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt  from volaris import (     bs_price,     mc_price_european, mc_price_asian, mc_price_barrier,     gbm_paths, gbm_paths_antithetic,     merton_paths,     garch_fit, garch_variances, garch_forecast, )  S, K, T, r, sigma = 100.0, 100.0, 1.0, 0.05, 0.20

title: Examples (Python)
text: Monte Carlo estimates option price simulating many asset paths averaging discounted payoffs. Comparison Black-Scholes analytical price:
code: bs_ref = bs_price(S, K, T, r, sigma, 1)  path_counts = [1_000, 5_000, 10_000, 50_000, 100_000, 500_000] prices, errors = [], []  for N in path_counts:     price, se = mc_price_european(S, K, T, r, sigma, N, 1)     prices.append(price)     errors.append(se) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))  ax.semilogx(path_counts, prices, "o-", label="Monte Carlo price") ax.fill_between(path_counts,                  [p - e for p, e in zip(prices, errors)],                 [p + e for p, e in zip(prices, errors)],                 alpha=0.25, label="standard deviation error") ax.axhline(bs_ref, color="red", ls="--", label=f"Black-Scholes = {bs_ref:.4f}") ax.set_xlabel("Number of paths N"); ax.set_ylabel("Price") ax.set_title("MC price convergence to Black-Scholes") ax.legend(); ax.grid(alpha=0.3)  plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

title: Examples (Python)
text: Exotic options depend full path, just terminal price: priced analytically simple closed form. Asian option: payoff based average price life option Barrier option: activated (knock-) deactivated (knock-) price crosses barrier B
code: N_paths, N_steps = 100_000, 252 B = 120.0  # barrier level  european, _ = mc_price_european(S, K, T, r, sigma, N_paths, 1) asian = mc_price_asian(S, K, T, r, sigma, N_paths, N_steps, 1) knockout = mc_price_barrier(S, K, T, r, sigma, N_paths, N_steps, B, 1, 1, 1) knockin = mc_price_barrier(S, K, T, r, sigma, N_paths, N_steps, B, 1, 0, 1)  print(f"European call:    {european:.4f}") print(f"Asian call:       {asian:.4f}") print(f"Up-and-out call:  {knockout:.4f}") print(f"Up-and-in call:   {knockin:.4f}") print(f"\nKnock-out + knock-in = {knockout + knockin:.4f}  (should more-less equal European = {european:.4f})") European call:    10.5372 Asian call:       5.7724 Up-and-out call:  1.3518 Up-and-in call:   9.1257  Knock-out + knock-in = 10.4775  (should more-less equal European = 10.5372)

title: Examples (Python)
text: Simulating asset paths Geometric Brownian Motion. Antithetic variates pair path mirror (-Z instead Z), reducing variance without additional model evaluations.
code: N_plot, N_steps = 200, 252  paths = gbm_paths(S, mu=0.05, sigma=sigma, T=1.0, N_steps=N_steps, N_paths=N_plot) paths_anti = gbm_paths_antithetic(S, mu=0.05, sigma=sigma, T=1.0, N_steps=N_steps, N_paths=N_plot) fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 6), sharey=True)  for p in paths[:50]:     axes[0].plot(p, alpha=0.7, lw=0.7, color="steelblue") axes[0].set_title("GBM paths"); axes[0].set_xlabel("Day"); axes[0].set_ylabel("Price") axes[0].axhline(S, ls="--", color="black")  for p in paths_anti[:50]:     axes[1].plot(p, alpha=0.7, lw=0.7, color="darkorange") axes[1].set_title("GBM paths: antithetic variates") axes[1].set_xlabel("Day") axes[1].axhline(S, ls="--", color="black")  plt.suptitle("GBM Simulation (S=100, mu=5%, sigma=20%, T=1 year)") plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

title: Examples (Python)
text: Merton model extends GBM adding compound Poisson jumps: sudden large moves GBM capture. Parameters: | Symbol | Meaning | |——–|———| | lambda | expected number jumps per year | | mu_j | mean log-jump size | | sigma_j | volatility log-jump size |
code: N_plot = 50  gbm_sim = gbm_paths(S, mu=0.05, sigma=0.15, T=1.0, N_steps=252, N_paths=N_plot) merton_sim = merton_paths(S, mu=0.05, sigma=0.15, lambda_=5.0, mu_j=-0.05,                            sigma_j=0.10, T=1.0, N_steps=252, N_paths=N_plot) fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 6), sharey=True)  for p in gbm_sim:     axes[0].plot(p, alpha=0.7, lw=0.7, color="steelblue") axes[0].set_title("GBM (no jumps)"); axes[0].set_xlabel("Day"); axes[0].set_ylabel("Price") axes[0].axhline(S, ls="--", color="black")  for p in merton_sim:     axes[1].plot(p, alpha=0.7, lw=0.7, color="crimson") axes[1].set_title("Merton jump-diffusion (lambda=5, mu_j=−5%, sigma_j=10%)") axes[1].set_xlabel("Day") axes[1].axhline(S, ls="--", color="black")  plt.suptitle("GBM vs Merton jump-diffusion") plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

title: Examples (Python)
text: GARCH(1,1) models volatility clustering: observation large moves tend follow large moves. fits time-varying variance: (sigma_t)^2 = omega + alpha * (eps_{t-1})^2 + beta * (sigma_{t+1})^2. simulate returns time-varying volatility, fit GARCH(1,1), forecast.
code: # Simulate returns with volatility clustering  np.random.seed(123) n = 1000 omega, alpha, beta = 0.0001, 0.10, 0.85  var = np.zeros(n) ret = np.zeros(n) var[0] = omega / (1 - alpha - beta) for t in range(1, n):     ret[t] = np.sqrt(var[t-1]) * np.random.randn()     var[t] = omega + alpha * ret[t]**2 + beta * var[t-1]  returns = np.ascontiguousarray(ret[1:]) # Fit GARCH(1,1) params = garch_fit(returns.tolist()) print(f"True:      omega={omega:.6f} | alpha={alpha:.6f} | beta={beta:.6f}") print(f"Fitted:    omega={params['omega']:.6f} | alpha={params['alpha']:.6f} | beta={params['beta']:.6f}")              variances = garch_variances(params['omega'], params['alpha'], params['beta'], returns.tolist()) forecast = garch_forecast(params['omega'], params['alpha'], params['beta'], returns.tolist(), 21) True:      omega=0.000100 | alpha=0.100000 | beta=0.850000 Fitted:    omega=0.000087 | alpha=0.077490 | beta=0.878328 fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(10, 10))  axes[0].plot(returns, lw=0.6, color="steelblue") axes[0].set_title("Simulated returns") axes[0].set_ylabel("Return") axes[0].grid(alpha=0.3)  axes[1].plot(np.sqrt(variances) * np.sqrt(252), lw=0.8, label="fitted sigma (annualised)") axes[1].plot(np.sqrt(var[1:]) * np.sqrt(252), lw=0.8, ls="--", label="true sigma") axes[1].set_title("GARCH(1,1) fitted vs true volatility") axes[1].set_ylabel("Annualised vol") axes[1].legend() axes[1].grid(alpha=0.5)  plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

title: Examples (R)
text: notebook demonstrates selection capabilities Volaris library: - option pricing Black-Scholes model - Greeks calculation: delta, gamma, vega, theta, rho - fast root-finding numerical integration compiled C core - binomial tree discrete alternative Black-Scholes

title: Examples (R)
text: Black-Scholes model assumes underlying option follows Geometric Brownian Motion. Variables used: | Symbol | Meaning | Example value | |——–|———|—————| | S | current price underlying asset | 100.0 | | K | strike price (exercise price option) | 100.0 | | T | time expiration years | 1.0 (= 1 year) | | r | risk-free interest rate (annualised) | 0.05 (= 5%) | | sigma | volatility underlying (annualised) | 0.20 (= 20%) | | C | call option price: right buy K | computed | | P | put option price: right sell K | computed | Call put prices --money option, verified via put-call parity:
code:
call <- bs_price(S, K, T, r, sigma, 1) put <- bs_price(S, K, T, r, sigma, 0) cat(sprintf("Call: %.4f
", call)) cat(sprintf("Put:  %.4f
", put)) cat(sprintf("Put-call parity check: %.4f vs %.4f
", call - put, S - K * exp(-r * T))) if (abs((call - put) - (S - K * exp(-r * T))) < 1e-10) cat("Put-call parity holds!
") Call: 10.4506 Put:  5.5735 Put-call parity check: 4.8771 vs 4.8771 Put-call parity holds!

title: Examples (R)
text: Greeks measure sensitivity option price market parameters. : delta, gamma, vega, theta call put options function spot price S.
code: spots <- seq(60, 140, length.out = 200) deltas_c <- sapply(spots, function(s) bs_delta(s, K, T, r, sigma, 1)) deltas_p <- sapply(spots, function(s) bs_delta(s, K, T, r, sigma, 0)) gammas <- sapply(spots, function(s) bs_gamma(s, K, T, r, sigma)) vegas <- sapply(spots, function(s) bs_vega(s, K, T, r, sigma)) thetas_c <- sapply(spots, function(s) bs_theta(s, K, T, r, sigma, 1)) thetas_p <- sapply(spots, function(s) bs_theta(s, K, T, r, sigma, 0)) par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1), oma = c(0, 0, 2, 0)) plot(spots, deltas_c, type="l", col="steelblue", xlab="S", ylab="", main="Delta") lines(spots, deltas_p, col="darkorange") abline(v=K, lty=2, col="gray") legend("topleft", c("call","put"), col=c("steelblue","darkorange"), lty=1, bty="n") grid() plot(spots, gammas, type="l", col="green4", xlab="S", ylab="", main="Gamma") abline(v=K, lty=2, col="gray") grid() plot(spots, vegas, type="l", col="red", xlab="S", ylab="", main="Vega") abline(v=K, lty=2, col="gray") grid() plot(spots, thetas_c, type="l", col="steelblue", xlab="S", ylab="", main="Theta", ylim=range(c(thetas_c, thetas_p))) lines(spots, thetas_p, col="darkorange") abline(v=K, lty=2, col="gray") legend("topright", c("call","put"), col=c("steelblue","darkorange"), lty=1, bty="n") grid() mtext("Greeks for an ATM option (K=100, T=1, r=5%, sigma=20%)", outer=TRUE, cex=1.1) par(mfrow=c(1,1))

title: Examples (R)
text: Volaris provides two root-finding methods implemented C. Example: find sigma BS call price equals 15. also exactly implied_vol internally.
code:
target <- 15.0 f <- function(sig) bs_price(S, K, T, r, sig, 1) - target df <- function(sig) bs_vega(S, K, T, r, sig) root_newton <- rootfind_newton(f, df, x0 = 0.3) root_bisect <- rootfind_bisect(f, 0.01, 2.0) iv <- implied_vol(target, S, K, T, r, 1) cat(sprintf("Target: %g
------------------
", target)) cat(sprintf("Newton-Raphson:  sigma = %.6f  |  price = %.6f
", root_newton, bs_price(S,K,T,r,root_newton,1))) cat(sprintf("Bisection:       sigma = %.6f  |  price = %.6f
", root_bisect, bs_price(S,K,T,r,root_bisect,1))) cat(sprintf("Implied vol:     sigma = %.6f  |  price = %.6f
", iv, bs_price(S,K,T,r,iv,1))) Target: 15 ------------------ Newton-Raphson:  sigma = 0.320258  |  price = 15.000000 Bisection:       sigma = 0.320258  |  price = 15.000000 Implied vol:     sigma = 0.320258  |  price = 15.000000

title: Examples (R)
text: Black-Scholes formula derived integral normal distribution. Comparison Gauss quadrature (custom C implementation) GSL QAGS adaptive integration:
code:
normal_pdf <- function(x) exp(-0.5 * x * x) / sqrt(2 * pi) a <- -3.0; b <- 3.0 exact <- 0.9973002039 result_gauss <- integrate_gauss(normal_pdf, a, b, n_points = 20L) result_gsl   <- integrate_gsl(normal_pdf, a, b, tol = 1e-10) cat(sprintf("Gauss quadrature (n=20): %.10f  |  error: %.2e
", result_gauss, abs(result_gauss - exact))) cat(sprintf("GSL QAGS:                %.10f  |  error: %.2e
", result_gsl, abs(result_gsl - exact))) cat(sprintf("Exact value:             %.10f
", exact)) Gauss quadrature (n=20): 0.9973002039  |  error: 3.67e-11 GSL QAGS:                0.9973002039  |  error: 3.67e-11 Exact value:             0.9973002039

what: Options pricing with a binomial tree
title: Examples (R)
text: Cox-Ross-Rubinstein model demonstrates Brownian motion binomial tree. number steps N increases, price converges Black-Scholes analytical value:
code: bs_ref <- bs_price(S, K, T, r, sigma, 1) steps <- c(5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500) prices <- sapply(steps, function(N) binomial_price(S, K, T, r, 0.0, sigma, N, 1, 0)) plot(steps, prices, log="x", type="b", pch=19, col="steelblue",       xlab="Number of steps N", ylab="Call option price",      main="Convergence of CRR binomial tree to Black-Scholes") abline(h=bs_ref, col="red", lty=2) legend("topright", c("Binomial tree", sprintf("Black-Scholes = %.4f", bs_ref)),        col=c("steelblue","red"), lty=c(1,2), pch=c(19,NA), bty="n"); grid()

title: Authors and Citation

title: Authors and Citation

code: @Manual{,   title = {Volaris: High-Performance Library for Quantitative Finance},   author = {Barbara Gawlik},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 1.0.2},   url = {https://allesgrau.github.io/volaris/}, }

title: High-Performance Library for Quantitative Finance
text: Volaris high-performance library quantitative finance – option pricing, volatility estimation, stochastic simulation, numerical methods, compiled C/C++ backend shared Python R. Whether pricing derivatives, fitting volatility models, running Monte Carlo simulations, Volaris gives C-speed without leaving Python R. Available : Python package – volaris (python/) R package – Volaris (R/Volaris/) Rendered example notebooks available project site: - Python – Black-Scholes, implied volatility, Monte Carlo pricing – Examples (Python) - R – Black-Scholes pricing – Examples (R) Source notebooks live notebooks/ (*.ipynb Python, *_r.ipynb R).

title: High-Performance Library for Quantitative Finance
text: Black-Scholes (Greeks calculation) Binomial Tree Monte Carlo (European, Asian, Barrier) Heston stochastic volatility Historical volatility (Close--Close, Parkinson, Garman-Klass, Yang-Zhang) Implied volatility GARCH(1,1) fit & forecast Geometric Brownian Motion (plain, antithetic) Merton jump-diffusion MCMC (Metropolis-Hastings) Root-finding (Newton-Raphson, Bisection) Numerical integration (Gauss quadrature, GSL adaptive)

title: High-Performance Library for Quantitative Finance
code: volaris/ │ ├── src/                            # Shared C/C++ source code │   ├── pricing/                    #   Black-Scholes, Binomial Tree, Heston, Monte Carlo │   ├── volatility/                 #   Historical vol, Implied vol, GARCH │   ├── simulation/                 #   GBM, Jump-Diffusion, MCMC │   ├── numerical/                  #   Root-finding, Integration │   └── utils/                      #   LCG random number generator │ ├── python/                         # Python package │   ├── volaris/ │   │   ├── _core.pyx               #   Cython wrapper (C modules) │   │   └── _heston_garch.pyx       #   Cython wrapper (C++ modules) │   ├── tests/ │   └── pyproject.toml │ ├── R/Volaris/                      # R package │   ├── R/                          #   R wrapper functions │   ├── src/                        #   C/C++ sources (copy from src/) + R wrappers │   │   ├── volaris_r.c             #     R/C API wrapper (C modules) │   │   └── heston_garch_rcpp.cpp   #     Rcpp wrapper (C++ modules) |   ├── man/                        #   Roxygen2-generated documentation |   ├── vignettes/                  #   Getting-started guide │   ├── tests/ |   ├── _pkgdown.yml                #   pkgdown site configuration │   └── DESCRIPTION │ ├── notebooks/                      # Jupyter notebooks with usage examples ├── .github/workflows/              # CI: R CMD check + pytest on Linux, macOS, Windows └── CHANGELOG.md

title: High-Performance Library for Quantitative Finance
text: C/C++ backend (required packages) GNU GSL >= 2.0: numerical integration OpenMP: parallelism Monte Carlo GARCH Python Python >= 3.9 NumPy >= 1.21 Cython R R >= 4.0.0 Rcpp Testing (optional) Python: pytest R: testthat

title: Binomial tree options pricing — binomial_price
text: S numeric scalar: current price underlying asset K numeric scalar: strike price option T numeric scalar: time expiration option (years) r numeric scalar: risk-free interest rate (annualized) q numeric scalar: continuous dividend yield underlying asset (annualized) sigma numeric scalar: volatility underlying asset (annualized) N integer scalar: number steps binomial tree is_call integer scalar: 1, price call option; 0, price put option is_american integer scalar: 1, price American option; 0, price European option

title: Binomial tree options pricing — binomial_price
text: Returns numeric scalar range (0, +inf) representing price option.

title: GBM parameter calibration via Metropolis-Hastings MCMC — mh_sampler_gbm
